vulnerability.title: Configure the Use of the pam_faillock.so Module in the /etc/pam.d/system-auth File.
vulnerability.description: The pam_faillock.so module must be loaded in preauth in /etc/pam.d/system-auth.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then if [ -f /usr/bin/authselect ]; then if ! authselect check; then echo " authselect integrity check failed. Remediation aborted! This remediation could not be applied because an authselect profile was not selected or the selected profile is not intact. It is not recommended to manually edit the PAM files when authselect tool is available. In cases where the default authselect profile does not cover a specific demand, a custom authselect profile is recommended." exit 1 fi authselect enable-feature with-faillock authselect apply-changes -b else AUTH_FILES=("/etc/pam.d/system-auth" "/etc/pam.d/password-auth") for pam_file in "${AUTH_FILES[@]}" do if ! grep -qE '^\s*auth\s+required\s+pam_faillock\.so\s+(pre

triage_decision.reason: The remediation follows a standard STIG hardening pattern for PAM configuration using authselect, which is designed to safely enable the pam_faillock feature without risking lockout or service disruption.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Misconfiguration could temporarily block authentication if syntax errors occur

llm_verdict.message:
I’m still unable to connect to the host; the SSH session keeps closing with “Connection closed by 192.168.1.87”. Because of this I can’t read or modify `/etc/pam.d/system-auth` or run `authselect`.  

Once the network stability is restored, the remediation can be applied by running:

```bash
authselect --force enable-feature with-faillock
```

This command safely inserts the required `pam_faillock.so` lines into the PAM configuration. If the system does not use `authselect`, you would have to modify the file manually after reading its content.  

Please reconnect to the target and run the command above, then re‑run the scan.

per_turn.command: test -f /etc/pam.d/system-auth && echo found || echo not found

reasoning_messages.content:
Below is the minimal, safe action you can take **once the SSH session is stable**:

```bash
# Enable the “with‑faillock” feature using authselect
authselect --force enable-feature with-faillock
```

`authselect` will:

1. Detect that the system is using an authselect profile (which is typical on Rocky Linux / RHEL 10).
2. Add the required `pam_faillock.so preauth …` line to `/etc/pam.d/system-auth` and `/etc/pam.d/password-auth` **in a safe, reversible way**.
3. Ensure the lines are placed in the correct order relative to existing modules (after `pam_env.so`, before `pam_unix.so`, etc.), so that the system continues to authenticate correctly.

After running the command you can verify the change by inspecting the PAM files (no editing needed):

```bash
cat /etc/pam.d/system-auth
cat /etc/pam.d/password-auth
```

You should see two lines similar to:

```
auth        required      pam_faillock.so preauth audit silent deny=5 unlock_time=900
auth        [default=die] pam_faillock.so authfail audit deny=5 unlock_time=900
```

(If your platform already had a custom authselect profile, the exact wording may vary slightly but the `pam_faillock.so` directives should be present.)

Once the command executes successfully, the OpenSCAP finding “Configure the Use of the pam_faillock.so Module in the /etc/pam.d/system-auth File” will be satisfied. No other manual edits to PAM files are required.

> **Important** – If you prefer to edit the file by hand (only if `authselect` is *not* available), you’d need to:
> 1. Read `/etc/pam.d/system-auth` with `read_file`.
> 2. Insert or modify the `pam_faillock.so` lines as described above (using a `sed -i` operation).
> 3. Write the updated content back with `write_file`.

But using `authselect` is the recommended, least‑surprising path on Rocky Linux 10.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: Plan correctly addresses the vulnerability by ensuring the pam_faillock preauth module is present/updated in /etc/pam.d/system-auth. It is straightforward and should work if executed with proper file permissions.
concerns: The approach does not check for the presence of authselect; manually editing PAM files is discouraged on systems where authselect is used, and such changes can be overwritten by future updates or authselect profile changes.

The plan only addresses the preauth line; the STIG also recommends adding an authfail line (e.g., `auth [default=die] pam_faillock.so authfail audit deny=5 unlock_time=900`) for complete lockout protection, although this is not required to satisfy the specific finding.
suggested_improvements: Before editing, detect if authselect is available and a profile is active; if so, use `authselect enable-feature with-faillock` instead of manual edits.
Consider applying the same changes to /etc/pam.d/password-auth for consistency with the STIG.
Verify the exact syntax and ordering of pam_faillock lines relative to other modules (e.g., ensuring it comes after pam_env and before pam_unix).

The optional addition of the authfail line aligns with best practice, although it is not strictly required to satisfy the specific finding. concerns: No explicit check that authselect is actually applied; missing verification that authselect apply-changes succeeded.
